headline: How Cosmos 3 Helps Physical AI Think Before It Acts
article_title: How Cosmos 3 Helps Physical AI Think Before It Acts

article_items.title: How Cosmos 3 Helps Physical AI Think Before It Acts
article_items.description: The new, open NVIDIA world foundation model brings vision reasoning, multimodal generation and action prediction together to help robots, autonomous vehicles and vision AI agents think before acting in the real world.
article_items.excerpt: The new, open NVIDIA world foundation model brings vision reasoning, multimodal generation and action prediction together to help robots, autonomous vehicles and vision AI agents think before acting in the real world.
article_items.summary: The new world foundation model - announced today at NVIDIA GTC Taipei at COMPUTEX - combines vision reasoning and multimodal generation across text, video, images, ambient sound and action in a single model to help developers create world data with physical context. Get Started With Cosmos 3 Developers can try Cosmos 3 on build.nvidia.com , download open models from Hugging Face , customize models and generate synthetic data with resources on GitHub , and deploy with NVIDIA NIM microservices. The NVIDIA GEAR team is using Cosmos 3 to develop video action models that help embodied agents learn how to reason, move and act across games, simulations and real-world robotics environments. The new, open NVIDIA world foundation model brings vision reasoning, multimodal generation and action prediction together to help robots, autonomous vehicles and vision AI agents think before acting in the real world. Reasoning Over Smart Cities and Spaces in Motion Cosmos 3 can reason across the scene and identify which objects are moving, where paths may intersect and what future state is likely to follow. Artificial Intelligence Inference ﻿ Agentic AI ﻿ Open Source ﻿ Generative AI ﻿ AI for Good ﻿.

key_points: The new world foundation model - announced today at NVIDIA GTC Taipei at COMPUTEX - combines vision reasoning and multimodal generation across text, video, images, ambient sound and action in a single model to help.

Get Started With Cosmos 3 Developers can try Cosmos 3 on build.nvidia.com , download open models from Hugging Face , customize models and generate synthetic data with resources on GitHub , and deploy with NVIDIA NIM.
The NVIDIA GEAR team is using Cosmos 3 to develop video action models that help embodied agents learn how to reason, move and act across games, simulations and real-world robotics environments.

The new, open NVIDIA world foundation model brings vision reasoning, multimodal generation and action prediction together to help robots, autonomous vehicles and vision AI agents think before acting in the real world.

Artificial Intelligence Inference ﻿ Agentic AI ﻿ Open Source ﻿ Generative AI ﻿ AI for Good ﻿ Research ﻿ AI Infrastructure AI Factory ﻿ Cloud ﻿ Hardware ﻿ Networking ﻿ Sovereign AI ﻿ Physical AI Robotics ﻿ Driving ﻿.
Cosmos 3 is the top-ranked open vision language model on VANTAGE-Bench that tests smart-infrastructure scene understanding and TAR challenge that tests traffic anomaly reasoning.

Generating Action Data for Real-World Robot Tasks Cosmos 3 is a generalist foundation model trained on diverse data that gives it a broad understanding of how scenes, motion and robotic actions relate.
Cosmos 3 can help generate physically plausible video sequences as a video foundation model to teach how the real world changes over time.
Cosmos 3 is also topping the Physics-IQ, R-Bench and PAI-Bench leaderboards, among other benchmarks for world generation.
Reasoning Over Smart Cities and Spaces in Motion Cosmos 3 can reason across the scene and identify which objects are moving, where paths may intersect and what future state is likely to follow.
With the OpenMDW 1.1 license from Linux Foundation, developers can use Cosmos model materials across physical AI workflows under a single, model-centric license.
The license makes it easier to train, modify, contribute, redistribute and deploy resources including weights, architecture, documentation, datasets, benchmarks and code.

article_items.title: Introducing Mellum2: A 12B Mixture-of-Experts Model by JetBrains
article_items.description: A Blog post by JetBrains on Hugging Face
article_items.excerpt: A Blog post by JetBrains on Hugging Face
article_items.summary: A Blog post by JetBrains on Hugging Face Introducing Mellum2: A 12B Mixture-of-Experts Model by JetBrains Mellum2 is a 12B-parameter Mixture-of-Experts model trained from scratch on natural language and code. Because Mellum2 is open and efficient to serve, it can be deployed in self-hosted environments involving proprietary code or internal data. Model architecture Mellum2 is a Mixture-of-Experts model: The MoE architecture keeps total model capacity high while activating only a subset of parameters for each token. With Mellum2, we extend that foundation to a broader set of natural language and software engineering tasks while keeping the model focused on efficient inference and deployability. Benchmark highlights In our technical report, we evaluate Mellum2 across code generation, reasoning, science, and math benchmarks. Model architecture Mellum2 is a Mixture-of-Experts model: The MoE architecture keeps total model capacity high while activating only a subset of parameters for each token.

Model architecture Mellum2 is a Mixture-of-Experts model: The MoE architecture keeps total model capacity high while activating only a subset of parameters for each token.
With Mellum2, we extend that foundation to a broader set of natural language and software engineering tasks while keeping the model focused on efficient inference and deployability.
The model activates only 2.5B parameters per token, making it efficient for high-throughput, low-latency inference.

Download the model on Hugging Face: For architecture details, training setup, benchmarks, and evaluation methodology, read the full technical report: Today we’re releasing Mellum2, an open Mixture-of-Experts model.
Models mentioned in this article 1 Collections mentioned in this article 1 Models mentioned in this article 1 Collections mentioned in this article 1

A single frontier model can be powerful, but production systems often need several specialized components working together: retrievers, routers, code-aware models, validators, tool callers, and larger reasoning models.
We think of Mellum2 as a “focal” model: a fast, well-scoped model optimized for high-frequency tasks inside larger AI systems.
This specialization keeps the model compact and efficient for software engineering workloads.
Because Mellum2 is open and efficient to serve, it can be deployed in self-hosted environments involving proprietary code or internal data.
Benchmark highlights In our technical report, we evaluate Mellum2 across code generation, reasoning, science, and math benchmarks.
The goal is not to replace every model in the stack.

article_items.title: ClawHub Security Signals: Large Corpus Multi-Scanner Dataset for Agent Skill Security Research
article_items.description: A Blog post by OpenClaw on Hugging Face
article_items.excerpt: A Blog post by OpenClaw on Hugging Face
article_items.summary: The goal of this release is to support researchers working on agent supply-chain security, multi-signal triage, and the class of risks that show up when AI agents install reusable skills and plugins. The release redacts secrets and excludes private identifiers and runnable private artifacts, but no automated redaction process is perfect. We hope to enable the research community and would love to help enable the development of safe agentic systems. It does not mean the skill is confirmed harmful. SkillSpector Risk Categories The top SkillSpector advisory categories are MCP Least Privilege (9,641 rows), MCP Tool Poisoning (5,084), Data Exfiltration (2,192), Dangerous Code Execution (1,629), Rogue Agent (1,428), and Supply Chain (1,336).
article_items.what_happened: The goal of this release is to support researchers working on agent supply-chain security, multi-signal triage, and the class of risks that show up when AI agents install reusable skills and plugins. The release redacts secrets and excludes private identifiers and runnable private artifacts, but no automated redaction process is perfect.
article_items.why_matters: We hope to enable the research community and would love to help enable the development of safe agentic systems. It does not mean the skill is confirmed harmful.
article_items.what_to_watch: SkillSpector Risk Categories The top SkillSpector advisory categories are MCP Least Privilege (9,641 rows), MCP Tool Poisoning (5,084), Data Exfiltration (2,192), Dangerous Code Execution (1,629), Rogue Agent (1,428), and Supply Chain (1,336).
key_points: The goal of this release is to support researchers working on agent supply-chain security, multi-signal triage, and the class of risks that show up when AI agents install reusable skills and plugins.

A Blog post by OpenClaw on Hugging Face ClawHub Security Signals: Large Corpus Multi-Scanner Dataset for Agent Skill Security Research Research Publication: ClawHub Security Signals: When VirusTotal, Static Analysis.

SkillSpector Risk Categories The top SkillSpector advisory categories are MCP Least Privilege (9,641 rows), MCP Tool Poisoning (5,084), Data Exfiltration (2,192), Dangerous Code Execution (1,629), Rogue Agent (1,428).

The 433 static findings for suspicious.prompt_injection_instructions and the 5,084 SkillSpector MCP Tool Poisoning rows provide a starting signal for evaluating detectors on realistic agent instruction content.
The release redacts secrets and excludes private identifiers and runnable private artifacts, but no automated redaction process is perfect.
Among suspicious rows, VirusTotal is positive on 12.7%, static analysis on 12.0%, and SkillSpector on 75.3%.
The dataset reflects the public ClawHub catalog at the time of export (31 May 2026 at time of publication).
Among malicious rows, VirusTotal is positive on 72.8%, static analysis on 12.6%, and SkillSpector on 6.8%.
The eval_holdout split is reserved for model evaluation and should not be used for training.
The verdict-conditioned pattern shows why a single scanner is not enough: Among clean rows, VirusTotal is positive on 4.4%, static analysis on 3.2%, and SkillSpector on 32.7%.
Only 468 rows, 0.69% of the dataset, are positive on all three scanner families.

Of the 35,600 rows with at least one positive scanner signal, 26,527 are SkillSpector-only: skills that do not raise VirusTotal or static-analysis positives but do raise semantic-risk advisories around authority.

article_items.title: AgentOps: Operationalize agentic AI at scale with Amazon Bedrock AgentCore | Amazon Web Services
article_items.description: When you build agentic AI solutions, you face unique operational challenges. Agents make unpredictable decisions, costs spiral unexpectedly, and debugging non-deterministic failures seems impossible. Agentic AI applications don't just execute predetermined workflows. They reason, adapt, and make autonomous decisions, and DevOps practices need to be adapted. That's where AgentOps comes in, the operational discipline for deploying, managing, and continuously improving AI agents in production.

article_items.title: Amazon Quick integration with time-series databases for market intelligence using MCP | Amazon Web Services
article_items.description: In this post, we walk through a practical implementation using KDB-X MCP server integration with Amazon Quick, demonstrating how traders and analysts can ask questions using conversational language and receive actionable insights from datasets. You can apply this same integration pattern across various domains, from financial market analysis to IoT sensor monitoring to DevOps performance dashboards, where you need to simplify access to time series insights.

rag_rules: Lead with the actual product, model, or API name.

article_items.title: Accelerate LLM model loading and increase context windows with GPUDirect on Amazon FSx for Lustre and TurboQuant | Amazon Web Services
article_items.description: If you’re iterating on deploying large language models (LLMs) on AWS GPU instances, you’ve probably noticed the larger the model to be loaded into GPU High Bandwidth Memory (HBM), the longer the painful wait until the GPUs are ready for inference. As models grow to hundreds of billions of parameters and GPU environments grow ever
